story:
“No wonder someone wants you dead.” Jasper Waldegrave lies facedown in the wet sand, drunk into oblivion, the stink of wine and rot curling my lip. I came to end him—the banker who fattens himself on the poor, steals their homes with predatory loans, and demands favors from girls when money runs dry. An easy death with a bottle feels too kind.

I nudge the bottle with my boot. It rolls away into the heavy darkness. In the distance, warm yellow lantern light illuminates the busiest parts of Ironport City, while taverns and brothels spill music into the cobblestone streets.

A tinkling echo draws my gaze into the darkness as the clouds part overhead, allowing the moon to peer down at the world below. The empty bottle I’d kicked moments before rolls back into view, and with it a familiar shadow emerges.

Rook—black on black, grin sharp as a hook—steps out as if we’d met on a crowded street rather than on the moonlit edge of Ironport. “My apologies. Was I interrupting, Lyra?” He purrs my name like a provocation.

“What do you want, Rook?”

I cross my arms but keep my hands well within reach of my daggers. With him, it’s difficult to know what to expect.

He crouches and rummages Jasper’s pockets with practiced hands. A signet glints between his fingers before he palms it away. “Relax. I earned this. Slipped him a draught with a tavern girl’s help. I’m here on a contract.”

“So am I.”

“From our illustrious Boss?” His smile widens. “Looks like they doubled the wager.”

“Neither of us is ceding the kill.”

“Didn’t think you would.”

I press the dagger to his throat to show him how serious I am—and that’s when lantern light and the clink of armor cut across the beach. Guards. I should have had more time. Tonight, the watch has shifted early.

“Go,” Rook says, low.

“Not without finishing it.” 

“By the gods, Lyra! Leave him!”

“No!”

With a barely restrained noise of frustration, Rook looks over my shoulder to judge the distance of the guards before they reach us. “Then… just do it. Do it now! Do it for Willa—this contract’s only your cover; you decided his death the day he locked you out.” The name spears through me.

The thought had already crossed my mind, but I hesitate. “And leave him here to be found with our footprints leading them right to us? We’d never escape! They’re too close.”

He rakes a shaking hand through his hair, looks desperately between me and the guards, and for a moment I think for sure he’s about to run. But he doesn’t. “Then help me drag him.”

“Where?” I grunt.

“The stone alcove. There’s nowhere else to hide out here.”

Just beyond my visibility, behind Rook, is the large stone alcove where the city’s waterways pour out from beneath the cliffs along the side of the beach. It’s likely where he’d come from and explains how he’d traversed so quietly in the dark. Further into the black expanse, the waterways become a series of endless tunnels never braved by the city’s guards.

We haul Jasper across the flats toward the stone alcove. We heave him onto a narrow ledge slick with algae and press flat in the alcove’s shadow as the patrol passes the place where we stood moments ago. The tide comes in with the watch, washing our tracks away until the beach shows no memory of us.

When the danger ebbs, Rook speaks into the hush, softer than I expect. “Why this job? Why risk the dungeons?”

I start loading rocks into Jasper’s pockets, jaw set. “He’s the reason my sister is dead.” The words cut fresh even now. “After our father died, Waldegrave promised help. Then he jacked the rent until we couldn’t pay. He threw us out into winter while I begged and she burned with fever.” The contract is only cover. His death was decided the day he locked us out.

The usual smugness falls from Rook’s face. Silence thickens.

Jasper groans.

In a lolling, half-conscious roll, he tips off the ledge into the filth-dark current. The splash is dull, swallowed by the tunnel. He sputters, flails—a drowning man trying to remember how to be a fish—then weakens. He dips, surfaces, dips again. The drug and the wine win. He goes slack and drifts toward open water with the city’s refuse, pockets heavier for the stones I managed to stuff in, too dazed to fight the pull.

We don’t touch him. We don’t help him. We don’t need to.

I wait for relief that never comes. A tear slips free. Rook catches it with his thumb as if we aren’t enemies.

“We remember you, Willa,” he says, and we watch the sea claim what it is owed. For one borrowed night, the man I spar with becomes a quiet harbor. In the morning, I’ll hate him again. But under moon and tide, with the city’s lanterns far away and one less monster breathing in Ironport, we share the only peace vengeance allows: not victory, but witness.
